content.text: is a blueprint that bundles data (attributes) and behaviour (methods) into a single named unit. An

— also called an instance — is a live, independent copy created from that blueprint, carrying its own private copy of the attributes. This model is the backbone of most production agent systems: sessions, API clients, tool wrappers, token budgets, and execution contexts are all naturally represented as objects.
of a concept: which pieces of data it holds and which operations it supports. Consider an

: every session needs a unique ID, a list of conversation turns, a model name, and a token counter. Defining these once in a class means every session object automatically has that structure, and the methods for manipulating it (
) live right next to the data they act on.
The class itself is never the thing that holds state — it is just the definition. Calling
asks Python to allocate memory, run
, and hand back a fully initialised
. Thousands of independent sessions can coexist because each object maintains its own copy of every instance attribute.

class AgentSession:
    def __init__(self, session_id: str, model: str = "gpt-4o"):
        self.session_id = session_id   # instance attribute
        self.model = model
        self.messages: list[dict] = []
        self.tokens_used = 0

    def add_message(self, role: str, content: str) -> None:
        self.messages.append({"role": role, "content": content})

    def token_count(self) -> int:
        return self.tokens_used

    def reset(self) -> None:
        self.messages.clear()
        self.tokens_used = 0

# Create two fully independent objects from the same class
session_a = AgentSession("run-001", model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022")
session_b = AgentSession("run-002")          # uses default model

session_a.add_message("user", "Hello")
print(len(session_a.messages))   # 1
print(len(session_b.messages))   # 0 — session_b is unaffected

Agent systems are inherently stateful. A single agent run accumulates messages, counts tokens, calls tools, tracks retries, and may branch into sub-agents. Without classes, this state lives in a jumble of parallel dictionaries or globals — which make it easy to forget fields, mutate the wrong variable, or pass incomplete structures across function boundaries.
: all state for a session travels together as one object, and the only way to interact with it is through its well-named methods. This means an
always appends to the right list, and every caller of
leaves the object in a known clean state rather than partially clearing disparate fields.
Polymorphism adds another layer of value: once you define a
method, the agent loop can call
without knowing whether the tool is a web-search wrapper, a database client, or a code executor — all that matters is the shared interface.
— data and the code that modifies it are collocated; callers cannot accidentally corrupt internal state without going through the defined interface.
once, instantiate as many as needed for concurrent users, batch jobs, or test harnesses.
— tool classes can share a common interface (
) so the dispatcher works without knowing the concrete type.
subclass that returns canned responses, then inject it into
— type-annotated attributes give autocomplete, jump-to-definition, and inline documentation for every
— a class that only holds three fields and no methods is usually better written as a
or a typed dict. Plain classes add
— if two parts of the code hold a reference to the same object and both mutate it, bugs become hard to trace. This is especially sharp in async agents where coroutines can interleave.
— not every helper needs a class hierarchy. A free function is often cleaner than a single-method class with no state.
— custom objects do not serialise to JSON or pickle automatically. You must define
or rely on a library like Pydantic.
— multiple levels of inheritance make code hard to follow. Prefer composition (holding objects as attributes) over deep inheritance chains.
statement it creates a class object and stores it under the given name. That class object is itself a callable: calling it runs
on a freshly allocated instance and returns the instance. Every subsequent call to
allocates a new block of memory with its own attribute dictionary (

class AgentSession:
    # Class attribute — lives on the class, shared across all instances
    DEFAULT_MODEL = "gpt-4o"

    def __init__(self, session_id: str, model: str | None = None):
        # Instance attributes — each object gets its own copy
        self.session_id = session_id
        self.model = model or AgentSession.DEFAULT_MODEL
        self.messages: list[dict] = []    # NEW list per instance
        self.tokens_used: int = 0

s1 = AgentSession("run-1")
s2 = AgentSession("run-2")
print(s1.__dict__)  # {'session_id': 'run-1', 'model': 'gpt-4o', 'messages': [], 'tokens_used': 0}
print(s1 is s2)     # False — different objects
). It is shared among all instances unless an instance shadows it by setting an attribute of the same name on itself. A
and belongs exclusively to that instance.
The most notorious trap: defining a mutable class attribute (a list or dict) that all instances unexpectedly share. Always initialise mutable containers inside

# WRONG: shared mutable class attribute
class BadSession:
    history = []            # <-- every instance shares this same list!

a = BadSession()
b = BadSession()
a.history.append("turn-1")
print(b.history)            # ['turn-1'] — b is polluted!

# CORRECT: initialise inside __init__
class GoodSession:
    def __init__(self):
        self.history = []   # new list for every instance

a = GoodSession()
b = GoodSession()
a.history.append("turn-1")
print(b.history)            # [] — isolated
Instance methods receive the instance as their first argument, conventionally named
is a plain reference to the calling object — you read and write its attributes through it.
Python's data model lets classes hook into built-in operations by defining
(double-underscore, also called magic methods). The most useful in agent code:
— makes the object callable like a function; great for tool wrappers

Use a class when an entity naturally bundles data that persists across multiple operations and methods that act on that data.
— message history, token counters, and metadata that grow across turns
— base URL, auth headers, retry logic, and rate-limit state
— configuration (top_k, timeout, API key) paired with a
— template strings, variable bindings, and a
— mutable work lists with enqueue/dequeue operations
Prefer plain functions or dataclasses when:
The data is read-only or structurally simple (use
There is no state to persist across calls (use a module-level function)
The operation is a pure transformation — input in, output out, nothing stored
A reliable workflow for authoring a class in an agent codebase:
. The name should be a noun that exactly describes the entity.
— write them as type-annotated assignments in
. Never leave an attribute unset in the constructor.
for bad inputs rather than letting bad state propagate.
— keep methods short and named after what they do, not how they do it.
— makes debugging and logging vastly easier.
— return types on methods prevent subtle bugs and enable IDE inference.
Example 1 — TokenBudget: a simple stateful guard
A minimal class that enforces a spending limit. The
spending so the limit is never exceeded — an invariant impossible to maintain with a bare integer.
A class that makes a retriever callable as a tool. The
method lets the agent dispatch the tool the same way it dispatches a plain function, while the class holds per-tool configuration (
) that would otherwise be implicit or global.
A production agent loop combines several objects.

owns a session, a budget, an LLM client, and a tool registry. Each turn it appends the user message, calls the LLM, charges the returned token count, optionally dispatches a tool call, and appends the assistant reply. Every piece of state lives in a named attribute, every operation has a named method, and the object can be reset or serialised without touching any globals.

from __future__ import annotations
import json

class AgentRunner:
    """Production agent loop: session + budget + LLM client + tools."""""

    def __init__(
        self,
        client,           # duck-typed: must have .complete(messages) -> Response
        session: AgentSession,
        budget: TokenBudget,
        tools: list | None = None,
        system_prompt: str = "You are a helpful assistant.",
    ) -> None:
        self.client = client
        self.session = session
        self.budget = budget
        self.tools: dict[str, object] = {t.name: t for t in (tools or [])}
        self._system = system_prompt

    def run(self, user_input: str) -> str:
        """Single user turn → assistant reply."""""
        self.session.add_message("user", user_input)
        messages = [
            {"role": "system", "content": self._system},
            *self.session.messages,
        ]
        tool_schemas = [t.schema for t in self.tools.values()]
        resp = self.client.complete(messages, tools=tool_schemas or None)

        if not self.budget.charge(resp.total_tokens):
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"Token budget exceeded: "
                f"{self.budget.spent + resp.total_tokens} > {self.budget.limit}"
            )

        # Handle tool calls
        if resp.tool_calls:
            for call in resp.tool_calls:
                tool_fn = self.tools.get(call.name)
                if tool_fn is None:
                    raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {call.name}")
                result = tool_fn(**json.loads(call.arguments))
                self.session.add_message("tool", json.dumps(result))
            # Second LLM pass to synthesise tool outputs
            resp = self.client.complete(
                [{"role": "system", "content": self._system},
                 *self.session.messages]
            )
            self.budget.charge(resp.total_tokens)

        self.session.add_message("assistant", resp.text)
        return resp.text

    def reset(self) -> None:
        self.session.reset()

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"AgentRunner(session={self.session.session_id!r}, "
            f"tools={list(self.tools)}, budget={self.budget})"
        )
for a stub, inject a pre-seeded
. No patching of globals, no side effects outside the object.
The most common Python OOP bug: a list or dict defined at class scope is shared across
instances. The fix is always to initialise mutable containers inside

# WRONG
class Session:
    messages = []   # shared! every instance appends to the same list

a = Session(); b = Session()
a.messages.append("hi")
print(b.messages)      # ['hi']  — BUG

# CORRECT
class Session:
    def __init__(self):
        self.messages = []   # fresh list per instance

a = Session(); b = Session()
a.messages.append("hi")
print(b.messages)      # []  — isolated
Putting the LLM client, tool registry, session state, logging, retries, and persistence all into one class creates a
: untestable, hard to change, impossible to reason about. Each concern should live in its own class that is composed into the runner.
reuses the same list across calls; use
instance to two concurrent async requests causes interleaved message appends. Create one session per request.
call chain becomes brittle. Prefer composition.
— session state, API client, tool registry, and token budget each get their own class.
— the object should be fully formed the moment the constructor returns. Never set attributes lazily in methods and then access them before they exist.
— do not make network calls, open files, or start threads in
should show the object's key identity, not
Type-annotate all attributes and return types
— enables mypy/pyright to catch type errors before runtime.
Validate in __init__, not in every method
once at construction rather than scattering checks through the class.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

# For data-heavy classes with little custom logic, @dataclass reduces boilerplate
@dataclass
class ToolResult:
    ok: bool
    data: object = None
    error: str | None = None
    tool_name: str = ""

    def to_message(self) -> dict:
        content = str(self.data) if self.ok else f"Error: {self.error}"
        return {"role": "tool", "content": content, "name": self.tool_name}

# @dataclass auto-generates __init__, __repr__, and __eq__
r = ToolResult(ok=True, data=["doc-1"], tool_name="search")
print(r)   # ToolResult(ok=True, data=['doc-1'], error=None, tool_name='search')
print(r.to_message())
Object instantiation in CPython is fast — creating an

is just an allocation and a few attribute assignments, taking under a microsecond. In an agent system that handles thousands of sessions per second, object creation is not the bottleneck; network I/O to the LLM API dominates by orders of magnitude.
The more relevant performance considerations are:

— each instance stores its attribute dictionary. A session holding 1,000 message dicts uses correspondingly more RAM. Limit message history by trimming old turns when approaching the context window limit.
— in async agent frameworks, multiple coroutines may reference the same tool object. If the tool holds per-call mutable state (a cursor, a buffer) it must be per-call-local, not stored as
— converting large session objects to JSON for persistence (Redis, PostgreSQL) dominates when scaling to many sessions. Use typed
methods and profile the conversion if sessions grow large.
— if you create millions of lightweight objects (tool call results, message wrappers), adding
to the class eliminates the per-instance dict and cuts memory roughly 40–60%.

class ToolResult:
    """High-volume result object — use __slots__ to reduce memory."""""
    __slots__ = ("ok", "data", "error")

    def __init__(self, ok: bool, data=None, error: str | None = None):
        self.ok = ok
        self.data = data
        self.error = error

import sys
class ToolResultDict:
    def __init__(self, ok, data=None, error=None):
        self.ok = ok; self.data = data; self.error = error

r1 = ToolResult(True, "hello")
r2 = ToolResultDict(True, "hello")
print(sys.getsizeof(r1))   # ~56 bytes (slots)
print(sys.getsizeof(r2))   # ~152 bytes (dict-backed)
The Python standard library and ecosystem provide several facilities that reduce boilerplate and extend the class model:
subclasses add runtime type validation, JSON serialisation, and schema generation. Ideal for LLM tool schemas and API payloads.
with more power (validators, converters, slot support).
— enforce that subclasses implement required methods; the standard pattern for
— structural subtyping (duck typing with type checking); define a
— static type checkers that understand class attribute types, method signatures, and
Classes are not the only way to structure agent code. The right choice depends on how much state and behaviour complexity the abstraction carries.
Data containers, few or no methods
Classes introduce several reliability risks that are specific to their mutable, shared-reference nature in agent systems.
— in a web-serving agent the same
instance must not be shared across concurrent HTTP requests. Each request must get its own
. Failing to isolate sessions leaks messages from one user to another.

— if an API key or auth token is stored on a class attribute rather than an instance attribute, every instance and every thread can read it. Store credentials in environment variables and load them once in the constructor.
Denial of service via unbounded lists
list grows without bound can exhaust memory. Add a
can execute arbitrary code. Never unpickle session data from untrusted sources; use
— Python's GIL does not protect composite operations like
if self.spent + n <= self.limit: self.spent += n
for shared mutable objects in threaded code, or ensure each coroutine owns its own object in async code.

import asyncio
import threading

# Async-safe: each coroutine gets its own session object — no locking needed
async def handle_request(session_id: str, user_input: str) -> str:
    session = AgentSession(session_id)   # new object per request
    runner = AgentRunner(client, session, TokenBudget(50_000))
    return await asyncio.to_thread(runner.run, user_input)

# Thread-safe shared budget (if truly shared across sessions)
class ThreadSafeTokenBudget:
    def __init__(self, limit: int) -> None:
        self.limit = limit
        self.spent = 0
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def charge(self, n: int) -> bool:
        with self._lock:            # atomic check-and-update
            if self.spent + n > self.limit:
                return False
            self.spent += n
            return True
Think of a class as a

. The cutter defines the shape (attributes) and what you can do with it (methods). Pressing the cutter into dough creates a new, fully formed cookie — the object — with the same shape but its own decoration (attribute values). One cutter makes thousands of independent cookies; changing the icing on one does not affect any other.
For composition, extend the analogy: an

that holds several cookies at once — a session cookie, a budget cookie, a client cookie. The plate is the composed object; the cookies it holds are the component objects. The plate does not become a cookie by holding one; it delegates:
For class attributes vs instance attributes, think of the cookie cutter's
(class attribute — shared, readable from any cookie baked by that cutter) versus each cookie's

(instance attribute — unique to that cookie). If the factory label were a list and one cookie pushed an item into it, the label on every other cookie would show that item too — the shared-mutable-list trap in one sentence.
A class is a blueprint defining attributes and methods; an object is a live instance with its own independent copy of every instance attribute.
— never define mutable defaults (lists, dicts) at class scope, because they are shared across every instance.
as their first argument; Python passes it automatically when you call
) hook into Python's built-in operations and make classes feel like first-class citizens of the language.
a session, a budget, and a client rather than inheriting from each.
Use classes when data and behaviour belong together and persist across multiple operations; use functions or dataclasses for stateless transforms or simple data containers.
Create one session object per request in concurrent systems — never share a mutable session between concurrent agents or HTTP requests.
reduce boilerplate and enforce contracts; choose the lightest abstraction that meets the need.
Object creation is cheap in CPython; the real costs are memory for large message lists, serialisation for persistence, and shared mutable state in concurrent agents.
